senses.definition: A supporter of the ideas of François-Noël Babeuf, especially equality and shared ownership of property.
examples: The historian described him as a babouvist in politics.
Babouvist ideas influenced later radical movements.

senses.definition: Connected with Babouvist ideas or supporters.
examples: The group took a babouvist position on land ownership.
Her paper explores babouvist thought in revolutionary France.

senses.definition: A wooden rack used by some Indigenous peoples in South America for drying or smoking food.
examples: The meat was placed on a babracot over the fire.
A babracot can be used for smoking fish or meat.

senses.definition: A root or root-like plant part; used as a rare or regional form in reference to a root vegetable.
examples: The term babroot is not common in modern English.
The plant produces a thick babroot underground.

senses.definition: An informal plural or affectionate form of bab or baby; also used as a nickname or name in some contexts.
examples: Come here, babs.
She is known as Babs among her friends.

senses.definition: A slang or informal term for women seen as shallow or overly focused on glamour and status.
examples: The article criticized a group of so-called babs.
He used babs as a rude label for the party guests.

senses.definition: In South Asia, a respectful title for a man, especially a clerk, educated man, or gentleman.
examples: The babu greeted the visitors politely.
He worked as a babu in the colonial office.

senses.definition: A term used in some South Asian settings for a child or son, often affectionate.
examples: The mother called her babu inside for dinner.
The little babu was asleep in the next room.

senses.definition: A South Asian form of address meaning a young master, boy, or young gentleman.
examples: Babua, come here at once.
The old servant called him babua out of respect.

senses.definition: A soft, slipper-like shoe or clog, especially one that is open at the back.
examples: She wore babouches with her loose trousers.
Traditional babouches are often brightly decorated.

senses.definition: Plural of babouche: soft slipper-like shoes, often traditional in North Africa or the Middle East.
examples: He bought a pair of babuches at the market.
The babuches were made of dyed leather.

senses.definition: The class, system, or influence of bureaucratic officials, especially in South Asia.
examples: Critics blamed babudom for slow reform.
The paper examines how babudom shaped administration.

senses.definition: Plural of babudom: systems or instances of bureaucratic rule by officials, especially in South Asian contexts.
examples: Different babudoms developed in the colonial period.
The report compared several babudoms across regions.

senses.definition: A rare form related to babu or baboon, depending on historical or local usage; not a common modern English word.
examples: The word babuina is rare in modern English.
He found babuina only in an old glossary.

senses.definition: The attitudes, practices, or influence associated with babus, especially bureaucratic behavior or official style.
examples: He criticized babuism in the administration.
The essay links babuism with slow decision-making.

senses.definition: Plural of babuism: attitudes or forms of bureaucratic behavior associated with babus.
examples: The article lists several babuisms in the system.
Old babuisms were hard to remove.

senses.definition: An acacia tree found in South Asia and Africa, often used for timber, gum, or shade.
examples: The babul grows well in dry areas.
Villagers use babul wood for fuel.

senses.definition: Plural of babul: acacia trees used in warm, dry regions.
examples: The road was lined with babuls.
Babuls can survive in poor soil.

senses.definition: A rare or local word form with unclear modern standard English use.
examples: Babuma is not a common English word.
Its meaning depends on local or historical usage.

senses.definition: A rare name or term with no widely established contemporary English sense.
examples: Babungera appears only in specialised or local sources.
The term babungera is not in common use.

senses.definition: A rare, nonstandard, or historical word form with no stable common modern meaning.
examples: Baburd is extremely rare in modern English.
The dictionary entry for baburd is uncertain.

senses.definition: Plural of babu: respected men, clerks, or officials in South Asian contexts.
examples: The babus met in the district office.
The novelist portrayed the babus as cautious administrators.

senses.definition: A headscarf tied under the chin, especially one worn by older women in Eastern Europe or Russia.
examples: She wore a babushka in the garden.
The grandmother covered her hair with a babushka.

senses.definition: An older Russian or Eastern European woman; often used with reference to a grandmother.
examples: The babushka smiled at the children.
They visited a kind babushka in the village.

senses.definition: Plural of babushka: headscarves or older women, especially in Russian or Eastern European contexts.
examples: The market sold colourful babushkas.
Several babushkas sat on the bench outside.

senses.definition: A very young child or an unborn child.
examples: The baby is sleeping.
She is expecting a baby in June.

senses.definition: A person or thing that is treated as very small, new, or weak.
examples: The company is still a baby in this market.
This project is a baby compared with our main product.

senses.definition: Very small, young, or less developed than usual.
examples: They bought baby carrots for the salad.
The baby bird could not fly yet.

senses.definition: Used in informal phrases to mean in a very small or not fully developed way.
examples: He is still acting babyish about the decision.
The plant is being grown baby-style indoors.

senses.definition: A short nightdress or loose dress with a high waist and a loose, flared shape.
examples: She wore a babydoll to bed.
The shop sells cotton babydolls in pastel colors.

senses.definition: An affectionate term for a pretty or attractive woman, sometimes patronizing.
examples: He called her babydoll in a playful tone.
The nickname babydoll can sound sweet or insulting.

senses.definition: Plural of babydoll: short nightdresses or loose dresses with a flared shape.
examples: The store displayed several babydolls.
Babydolls are often sold with matching shorts.

senses.definition: The state or world of babies and very young children.
examples: The book explores the demands of babydom.
Life in babydom revolves around feeding and sleep.
